narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive determination in pursuing your legal dreams despite the challenges many face in this field. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is right in line with the typical student's, and your family comes from a middle-income background, which is common among applicants. You ranked in the 8th decile for your first-year school grades—stronger than average—and 6th decile by your third year, also slightly above typical. However, your LSAT score of 30 is below the usual 36.7 average, and you've indicated plans to attend part-time, which is less common as most students go full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 40% chance you'll face failure on that critical test—a risk that unfortunately tipped the scales toward rejecting your application. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your part-time status, as the model sees full-time students as having a clear edge in bar preparation and success rates. On a positive note, your third-year decile rank strongly pulled the prediction toward passing, showing your academic momentum. Your first-year decile did the same, highlighting early strengths that buck typical trends. Your solid undergraduate GPA also helped nudge things toward success, while family income played a smaller supportive role. That slightly lower LSAT had a minor countereffect, but it wasn't the main driver. Compared to typical students, your grades stand out favorably, yet the part-time choice outweighed them in the model's eyes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for a boost, exploring full-time options if feasible, or strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework—many resilient applicants like you succeed by addressing these key areas and reapplying stronger. You've got real potential; this is just one data point in your journey. Keep pushing.
